For example, I point you to the copper volatility of 2021-2022, followed by the 2022-2023 crash, when Lundin fell to 6-7x P/E, compared to 30x P/E.

The fact that the estimated rate of annualized return from here on is negative 20%+ until 2027E, based on a growth rate of double digits here, is dangerous.

How do I come up with this?

I use the 5-year average P/E, which is significantly higher than the 20-year average. I forecast this based on current estimated growth rates, and we arrive at an annualized return of negative 12% even with the yield, and a share price of $21/share.

Even if you were to forecast Lundin Mining at something like a 30x P/E here, you'd only get 11% per year out of that based on these surge-like forecasts. That is not a good rate of return.

Peers to the company are many, and many have similar types of focus. I'll go through a couple of them here. Capstone Copper (CS:CA) is a pure-play copper and also with the same sort of volatility and trend. I'd say "no" here, unless you're willing to take a significant risk following almost 15 years of underperformance. Freeport (FCX) is another pure-play copper with a native US listing, but its trends are almost identical to Lundin. All in all, the entire sector shows us its overvalued face here, as well as the fact that outside of this overvaluation, it's a volatile, prone-to-misses sort of place with significant risk.

I do not view any of these peers as appealingly valued or attractive investments here. The upside in copper has played itself out, in my view, and the only thing remaining here is either negative RoR or risk.

On the basis of this, I review my PT and fair value target for Lundin. The previous target was $15.5/share. I'd allow a 17-18x P/E upside for this company, discounting for the company's lack of forecast accuracy, while allowing higher growth than companies tied to more legacy-type assets in more conservative Geographies. A $20-$21/share fair value is possible, which would make a PT of about $16.5/share worthy of an upside, representing a slight bump from my last article.

Other analysts have the company at a low of about $14.2, to a high of about $23/share, with an average of $20.19 - so about the same fair value as I have, perhaps a bit higher. Given that 11 out of 22 analysts are at "BUY", the ratings are somewhat split - but I would argue there's an insufficient amount of caution here given the levels the company is currently trading at, relative to targets (and not a single analyst being above $23.28/share.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

My "HOLD" rating for Lundin Mining has the risk of the company outperforming averages, or the stock outperforming as it has in the past. The company's asset sales from an international perspective have given the company stability that could result in higher returns and results. If this were to happen, the price target needs to go up. The continued JV's could also increase the company's counter-cyclical resilience, and this is hard to estimate.

However, I believe the company is better suited for a "HOLD" here, especially given the latest trends and what could be expected from the lack of upside following this surge.

Thesis

Lundin Mining is a complex mining company with a solid long-term upside, owned 15%+ by the billionaire Lundin Family out of Sweden. The company operates mining assets across the world, with a focus on South America, which represents more than 80% of revenue and profit.

I believe the company is likely to be one of the major copper players on a forward basis, and I believe, at the right value, the company is worth investing in. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a fair value target of $20/share, which in turn comes to a $16.5/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a PT of $16.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "HOLD" rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: Still Undervalued And Generating 4.65% Dividend Yield
data.description:
It’s been a great end of the year for Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) as shares have rallied over 27% since October from $42.52 to $54.19. I believe that BMY is still a misunderstood company as its been looked at as a value trap for several years. The investment community seems to be more focused on what is rolling off patent than what is ramping up. After going through the Q3 results I think this narrative is incorrect and BMY looks less like a company that is in a managed decline and more like a company in transition as its growing its slate of newer brands and late-stage assets. Over time I believe that BMY can offset the revenue loss from assets coming off patent which should help the market change its narrative. The reality is that BMY is generating over $15 billion in free cash flow (FCF), has strong financials, is back to profitability, and growing the dividend. BMY still trades at a single digit P/E with a dividend yield of 4.65%. I am very bullish on BMY as I feel it’s an overlooked opportunity for both yield and capital appreciation. As the risk-free rate of return declines BMY’s growing dividend is likely to look more appealing and the lower Fed Effective Rate could create a situation where BMY is able to expand margins by issuing new debt to pay off higher yielding existing debt. It’s been a disappointing decade for BMY shareholders but if the broad market rallies in 2026 BMY is certainly a candidate that can capitalize on that opportunity.

Following up on my previous article about Bristol Myers

At the beginning of August I wrote an article about BMY (can be read here) discussing why I felt it was undervalued. BMY delivered a double beat on their Q2 earnings and raised their year end guidance as they continued to execute a successful transition from legacy drugs to a growth portfolio. I believed that BMY added several strategic partnerships in Q2 which made it a contrarian value and income play. I am following up with a new article because I still believe that the market is discounting BMY’s future cash flow. BMY is trading at 8.29 times 2025 earnings and paying a dividend yield of 4.65%. BMY is generating over $15 billion in annualized FCF and has a strong enough balance sheet to continue transitioning toward prospective growth projects. I think that BMY will continue to deliver strong dividend growth and capital appreciation for long-term investors.

BMY’s Q3 results solidified their transition to future growth

In Q3 BMY delivered $12.22 billion in revenue which was an increase of 3% YoY while beating the consensus estimates by $422.1 million. BMY was able to generate $1.63 of EPS which came in $0.11 higher than what The Street was looking for. Q3 showcased to the market that all the investments that BMY has made is paying off. It’s growth portfolio generated $6.9 billion in revenue which was an increase of 18% YoY while the legacy drug portfolio declined -12% YoY to $5.4 billion in revenue. The strength from the growth portfolio was enough to offset the declining revenue from its previous drugs while allowing BMY to increase its 2025 projections to roughly $47.5–$48.0 billion of revenue while guiding for non-GAAP EPS between $6.40–$6.60. BMY is showing the market that it is able to build future durable income through its diversified growth portfolio. The theme in Q3 wasn’t that growth was outperforming the legacy portfolio even if that’s the way it looked on the surface. BMY has multiple products that are compounding at the same time which reduces dependency on one product which improves and diversifies its revenue mix.

When I look at the oncology portfolio it remained a cornerstone for BMY generating $2.53 billion which was up 7% YoY. This was aided by momentum in Yervoy which produced $739 million in revenue up 15% YoY and Opdualag which produced $299 million in revenue which was up 28% YoY. BMY’s oncology segment isn’t focused on one product as their working on several regimens, combinations, and indications that can fortify its revenue mix in an environment where competition is getting more fierce. BMY watched Reblozyl’s revenue increase 37% YoY to $615 million while Breyanzi increased 60% YoY to $359 million. While the market is looking at BMY as a company that is in transition I am seeing them as a company that is scaling several compounds from their infancy to being real contributors to the top and bottom line.

I believe that BMY is delivering on its strategy of accelerating its growth portfolio, diversifying its pipeline through business development, and maintaining balance sheet flexibility. BMY has leaned into strategic transactions to diversify future growth drivers. In October 2025 BMY entered into an agreement to acquire Orbital Therapeutics for $1.5 billion in cash which provided diversification into next generation platforms which included RNA enables compounds. BMY also entered into a partnership with BioNTech where BMY gained access to an oncology asset BNT327. This has allowed BMY’s revenue mix to be fortified by the growth portfolio as the strategic pivot in BMY’s product mix includes oncology, cardiovascular, immunology. and neuroscience. BMY finished Q3 with $16.5 billion in cash and short-term investments while having paid more than $5 billion of its long-term debt off since Q1 2024. BMY has the flexibility in its balance sheet and cash flow to continue raising the dividend and buying back stock from the $5 billion left on the authorization.

I believe that BMY has positioned themselves for the future as they have constructed the growth portfolio to have enough clinical catalysts to manage the legacy portfolios decline. This is a step to returning toward sticky growth as the growth portfolio growing by 18% YoY has transcended incremental. BMY is also entering into a data rich period with multiple catalysts across CNS, immunology, cardiovascular, hematology, and oncology. The market could rerate BMY if these products can become ongoing revenue streams. BMY flagged Cobenfy Alzheimer’s Disease Psychosis and Cobenfy Bipolar-1 as major upcoming catalysts. CNS can help unlock value for BMY because it could expand BMY’s footprint. BMY could also recognize a lot of value in immunology through Sotyktu. If BMY experiences successful expansions it would transform Sotyktu from a single-indication growth asset into a broader immunology franchise. BMY has the ability to get rerated if several of these milestones turn into category drivers.

I still see a lot of value in shares of BMY

CME Group is projecting that we are going to get 2 rate cuts in 2026 taking the target rate down to 300-325 bps. BMY is paying a dividend of $2.52 per share which is a 4.65% yield in an environment where the risk-free rate of return is declining. BMY has been able to increased the dividend for 17 years with an average growth rate of 6.62% over the past 5-years. BMY has a 37.80% payout ratio and I believe they will continue increasing the dividend considering they generate in excess of $15 billion in FCF annually. As rates continue to fall BMY’s dividend yield will look more and more enticing and I believe that locking in the yield here will be a beneficial for investors going forward.

I compared BMY to Pfizer (PFE), GSK plc (GSK), Sanofi (SNY), and Merck & CO (MRK) and when I looked at the numbers BMY looks very undervalued. I looked at price to EBITDA, operating income, cash from operations, FCF, and net income to determine if BMY is expensive or not. Over the TTM BMY has generated $19.22 billion in EBITDA, $14.32 billion in operating income, $16.62 billion in cash from ops, $15.3 billion in FCF, and $6 billion in net income. When I compare the multiples on each of these metrics BMY has the lowest valuation on 4 of these 5 metrics. BMY trades at 5.74 times EBITDA, 7.71 times operating income, 6.64 times operating income, and 7.21 times FCF. None of its peers that I listed trade at a multiple of less than 10 times FCF. I think that the level of profitability that BMY generates is being discounted and there is an opportunity for unlocking value at these levels.

Risks to investing in BMY

While I am bullish on BMY there are several risk factors to consider. BMY has a legacy portfolio that is eroding in market share. With declining revenue from its legacy products the market is likely to be skeptical if the growth side of its business will be able to offset a continuation in losses from products coming off patent. BMY also has a risk in pricing due to the political scrutiny about Medicare. If reimbursement levels change then BMY could experience compressed margins and less profitability in the future. There is also no guarantee that its pipeline of prospective products will make it out of clinical trials. For every product that fails it’s a loss of not just invested capital but time as well. Pharmaceuticals are difficult because anything can happen in the clinical trials that can derail long-term projects. Investors should do their own due diligence as investing in BMY comes with many large risks.

Conclusion

BMY is undergoing a transition where its growth portfolio is starting to scale and outpace its declining legacy portfolio. I believe that the legacy portfolio will continue to scale and allow BMY to increase its profitability over time making the current valuations look inexpensive. If this occurs the market could rerate shares of BMY as they would see less risk from drugs coming off patent. BMY trading at less than 8 times FCF, cash from operations, operating income, and EBITDA is a valuation that I am not passing on. Investors are able to lock in a 4.6% dividend yield at these levels while getting BMY at an inexpensive valuation. Over the next year I believe that BMY will look more like a growth story than a legacy drug maker on the decline and investors may be able to unlock a lot of value at these levels while getting paid a large dividend at a time when the risk-free rate of return is declining.
